# Back to the serial interval

## Now think of it as a composed delay

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="38%"}
- We treated the SI as one event-to-event delay; it can be thought of
  as a **composition** of delays
- Onsets are what we see; infection and transmission are latent

$$ GT = LP + U $$
$$ SI = GT + (IP_2 - IP_1) $$

- The composition is where the biology can enter
:::
::: {.column width="62%"}
![](figures/fig-gt-vs-si-timeline.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Source: within-host informed event-time framework (Jamieson et al.,
working paper)
:::
:::
::::

# A within-host informed approach

## One within-host model coupling the delays

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="38%"}
- A shared pathogen load $V(t)$ in the infector drives three event
  hazards
  - infectiousness onset $h_I$
  - symptom onset $h_S$
  - transmission $p_{\text{trans}}$
- So $LP$, $U$, and $IP_1$ are **coupled**, not separate marginals
- The delays stay mutually consistent, and the parameters keep a
  biological meaning
- Builds on the within-host to Burr-distribution idea of
  [Jamieson & Hall (2024)](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1012041)
:::
::: {.column width="62%"}
![](figures/fig-event-time-dag.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Framework: Jamieson et al. (working paper). Within-host idea:
[Jamieson et al. (2024)](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1012041)
:::
:::
::::

## The viral-load trajectory

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="42%"}
- A rise-and-fall pathogen load with four interpretable parameters
  - growth rate $r$
  - peak time $m$
  - decay rate $d$
  - transition sharpness $\kappa$

$$
V(t) = A\, e^{rt}
\left\{1 + \tfrac{r}{d}\,e^{\kappa(t-m)}\right\}^{-(r+d)/\kappa}
$$
:::
::: {.column width="58%"}
![](figures/pathogen_load_varying_kappa_peak_at_m.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Framework: Jamieson et al. (working paper). Trajectory form follows
[Ke et al. 2021](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2111477118),
[Puhach et al. 2023](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41579-022-00822-w)
:::
:::
::::

## Per-event hazards turn load into delays

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Each event is a stochastic activation with a hazard set by the load

$$
F_Z(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left\{-\!\int_0^t h_Z(s)\,ds\right\}
$$

- $V(t)$ is shaped by $r, m, d, \kappa$; a per-event scale $\beta_Z$
  sets how strongly each event responds to load
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
Each delay reads off the same $V(t)$:

infectiousness onset $\to LP$
$$ h_I(t) = \beta_I\, V(t) $$

symptom onset $\to IP$
$$ h_S(t) = \beta_S\, V(t) $$

transmission $\to U$ (so $GT = LP + U$)
$$ \lambda_T(u \mid LP{=}\ell) = c\,\beta_T\, V(\ell + u) $$
:::
::::

::: {.callout-note}
**Why these forms?** Each rate is taken proportional to load as a
parsimonious baseline; other activation functions give other families
(a separable $V(t)\,g(t)$ gives the Burr).

- $h_S = \beta_S V(t)$: symptoms have no fixed load threshold — they
  track replication, host response, and detection — so onset is a
  stochastic activation that rises with load
- $\lambda_T = c\,\beta_T V(t)$: transmission needs a **contact**
  (rate $c$) and infection given contact (probability rising with
  shedding, so with load); $c$ is held constant for parsimony
:::

::: {.attribution}
Framework: Jamieson et al. (working paper)
:::

## SARS-CoV-2: shorter generation time for Omicron

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
- Dutch household transmission pairs, Delta replaced by Omicron
- Shorter mean generation time for Omicron than Delta
- Lower WAIC and LOOIC than a lognormal generation-time model, for
  both strains
- Timing variation is allocated to interpretable components
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](figures/figure3_generation_mean_sd_lognormal_vs_updatedV_obspair.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Framework: Jamieson et al. (working paper). Data:
[Backer et al. 2022](https://doi.org/10.2807/1560-7917.ES.2022.27.6.2200042),
[Park et al. 2023](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2221887120)
:::
:::
::::

## SARS-CoV-2: fit to the serial intervals

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
- Both models reproduce the modal interval and the tails
- For Omicron the within-host informed model captures the peak and
  both tails better than the lognormal
- The fit stays comparable while keeping a biological link to the
  latent generation time
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](figures/figure_serial_fit_overlay_lognormal_vs_WHinformed_omicron_delta.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Framework: Jamieson et al. (working paper)
:::
:::
::::

## Mpox: bringing in external viral-load information

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
- 34 high-confidence mpox transmission pairs
- External viral-load data build informative priors on the trajectory
  parameters $(\log r, \log d, m, \log\kappa)$
- The decline $\log d$ and peak time $m$ shift under the viral-load
  prior: external information constrains weakly identified parameters
- A natural next step: **jointly fit** the transmission pairs and the
  viral-load data, rather than passing one in as a prior
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](figures/WH_prior_posterior_comparison_logr_logd_logkappa_m.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Framework: Jamieson et al. (working paper). Mpox pairs:
[Miura et al. 2024](https://doi.org/10.1093/infdis/jiad091);
viral load:
[Yang et al. 2024](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-024-48754-8)
:::
:::
::::

# You don't need a within-host model

## The hierarchy is agnostic to the hazard

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
- The event-time hierarchy holds for any hazard form; the within-host
  $V(t)$ is one choice
- A generic alternative: **contact-interval survival models** — time
  from infectiousness onset to infectious contact is a transmission
  hazard, with no within-host trajectory needed
  ([Kenah 2011](https://doi.org/10.1093/biostatistics/kxq068))
- Contact intervals are right-censored, so survival analysis is the
  natural tool, and the natural meeting point with this room
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](figures/fig-contact-interval.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Diagram: this talk, after the contact-interval idea of
[Kenah (2011)](https://doi.org/10.1093/biostatistics/kxq068)
:::
:::
::::
